issues.title: Live product code still splits pipeline policy imports across canonical and compatibility paths
issues.summary: The docs describe `common/pipeline_policy.py` as a compatibility import surface for `common/orchestration_policy.py`, but mounted API and worker code still import from both paths directly. That keeps two live ownership paths for the same ingest/orchestration policy helpers.
issues.impact: Pipeline-policy ownership is still ambiguous in live code, not just in docs. That raises the cost of changing NAT gate or orchestration behavior because reviewers must check both import surfaces to know which path is actually canonical.
issues.notes: The docs describe `common/pipeline_policy.py` as a compatibility import surface for `common/orchestration_policy.py`, but mounted API and worker code still import from both paths directly. That keeps two live ownershi...

issues.title: Mounted artifact visibility resolver still crashes on valid artifact IDs
issues.summary: `POST /api/artifacts/visibility/resolve` resolves visible `Artifact` rows correctly, but then calls `get_batch_for_artifact(db, artifact.artifact_id)` with a UUID/string instead of the artifact model. The shared helper immediately dereferences `artifact.batch`, so any request containing at least one visible artifact can raise an attribute error instead of returning scoped summaries.
issues.impact: The mounted visibility-resolution helper is live and can fail on valid requests, so persisted artifact-reference surfaces cannot reliably rehydrate visible artifact cards under case or bucket scope. Instead of hiding out-of-scope items and returning the rest, the route can 500 as soon as it hits the first visible artifact.

issues.title: Fast Summary still marks reports done when every per-file summary is just a backend failure string
issues.summary: The mounted Report Studio exposes `FAST_SUMMARY`, and the worker treats any string returned by `gemma_processor.summarize()` as valid report content. But the Gemma wrapper returns literal status/error text such as `Skipped (Offline Mode)`, `Error: Model not loaded.`, and `Summarization failed: ...` instead of raising. `ReportProcessor.process()` then stores that content and marks the report `DONE`.
issues.impact: In air-gapped or misconfigured deployments, analysts can run a Fast Summary report and receive a `DONE` report whose body is mostly backend failure text rather than document summaries. That makes report status untrustworthy and forces analysts to manually detect generation failure from the prose itself.
issues.notes: Fast Summary still marks reports done when every per-file summary is just a backend failure string

issues.title: Embedding job output validation still treats missing retrieval-layer families as success
issues.summary: `GENERATE_EMBEDDING` declares that it produces embeddings for `raw_retrieval`, `ocr_retrieval`, and `translated_retrieval` chunk families, but the shared validator returns success when a family has zero rows at all. That means the dispatcher can mark the job `DONE` without proving those chunk-level outputs exist, even though downstream semantic search reads chunk embeddings from those retrieval-layer ranges.
issues.impact: The runtime can report embedding generation as successful while never proving chunk-level embedding coverage for one or more retrieval families. That weakens the trustworthiness of the embedding job state and can leave downstream semantic evidence search operating on a partially embedded corpus without any contract failure surfacing it.
issues.notes: Embedding job output validation still treats missing retrieval-layer families as success

issues.title: Local Fast Summary fallback still attempts runtime model downloads in air-gapped deployments
issues.summary: The mounted Report Studio still advertises `Fast Summary (Gemma)`. When the summary backend is not `openai`, the Gemma loader falls back to Hugging Face `from_pretrained(...)` and only skips that path if `OFFLINE_MODE` is explicitly enabled. If the local cache is missing and the deployment is simply air-gapped rather than configured with `OFFLINE_MODE=true`, the fallback path can still attempt a runtime download.
issues.impact: A deployment that selects the local/transformers summary lane but relies on the repository’s normal air-gapped discipline can still hit outbound model-resolution attempts or long timeout failures at report time when Gemma is not already cached. That breaks the stated local-first contract and makes Fast Summary behavior depend on an extra `OFFLINE_MODE` flag rather than the actual deployment model-cache state.
issues.notes: Local Fast Summary fallback still attempts runtime model downloads in air-gapped deployments

issues.title: Canonical Windows AI service still ignores repo model-download guards and can fetch models at runtime
issues.summary: `services/windows_ai/server.py` is still documented as a canonical runtime endpoint, but its translator and embedder loaders call `from_pretrained(...)` / `SentenceTransformer(...)` directly from model IDs and do not consult `ALLOW_MODEL_DOWNLOADS`, `OFFLINE_MODE`, or `MODEL_CACHE_DIR`. In an air-gapped deployment without those models already present in the local Hugging Face cache, the service can still attempt outbound model resolution at runtime.
issues.impact: The Windows AI node can violate the repo’s air-gapped/local-cache contract even when the main worker/runtime is configured to disallow downloads. That leaves translation and embedding behavior on this canonical service dependent on ambient Hugging Face cache state rather than the repository’s declared runtime policy.
issues.notes: Canonical Windows AI service still ignores repo model-download guards and can fetch models at runtime

issues.title: Runtime flag reload still hard-fails when optional NLP dependencies are absent
issues.summary: `POST /api/ops/feature-flags/reload` imports `worker.src.processors.nlp` directly just to refresh runtime NLP configuration. That module imports `transformers` at module import time with no guard, so deployments that do not ship the optional NER stack can get a 500 from a flag-cache reload endpoint even though the rest of the reload work is unrelated.
issues.impact: A live ops-control endpoint can fail entirely in lean or partially provisioned deployments where NER libraries are intentionally absent, even though flag reload, LLM cache invalidation, and embedding runtime refresh should still succeed. That couples a broad runtime-maintenance action to one optional model family and makes operational recovery more brittle than the guarded readiness path.
issues.notes: Runtime flag reload still hard-fails when optional NLP dependencies are absent

issues.title: Canonical Windows AI embedding endpoint still emits 384-D vectors against a 1024-D repo contract
issues.summary: The documented Windows GPU service exposes `/embed`, but it hard-wires `SentenceTransformer("all-MiniLM-L6-v2")`, which produces 384-dimensional vectors. The main CoTitan runtime and schema are configured around 1024-D embeddings, with `Artifact.embedding`, `Chunk.embedding`, and related strict-dimension logic all expecting that contract.
issues.impact: Any runtime path or operator workflow that treats the canonical Windows AI `/embed` endpoint as compatible with the main embedding pipeline will get vectors with the wrong dimensionality. That can break pgvector writes/searches outright or force ad hoc padding/truncation that no documented contract currently declares.
issues.notes: Canonical Windows AI embedding endpoint still emits 384-D vectors against a 1024-D repo contract

issues.title: Canonical native Surya OCR service still drops auth entirely when no key env is set
issues.summary: `services/native_vision/surya_server.py` only attaches the `X-API-Key` dependency if one of `SURYA_API_KEY`, `TITAN_AI_KEY`, or `API_KEY` is present. If none is configured, the documented canonical external OCR runtime starts with no auth dependency at all and every endpoint becomes publicly callable.
issues.impact: A canonical external OCR boundary can be exposed without any authentication simply because a deployment forgot to set one env var. That turns the Surya health/runtime/ocr endpoints into unauthenticated remote surfaces instead of failing closed, which is a live service-boundary bug rather than just a documentation mismatch.

issues.title: Canonical native Surya OCR service still echoes its configured API key back to callers
issues.summary: `build_surya_runtime_spec()` passes the configured `SURYA_API_KEY` into `build_native_vision_runtime()`, whose dataclass includes `api_key` and serializes it verbatim via `asdict()`. That runtime dict is then returned from `/healthz`, `/vision/runtime`, and successful `/ocr/surya` responses.
issues.impact: Any caller that can hit the canonical native Surya service can recover the service's own configured API key from ordinary health/runtime/OCR responses. Combined with the fail-open auth path already logged above, this turns a service-local shared secret into response data instead of a credential boundary.

issues.title: Canonical Windows AI embedding endpoint still does not match the shared remote-embedding contract
issues.summary: The repo documents `services/windows_ai/server.py` as the canonical Windows AI endpoint, but the shared embedding client and model warmup only speak an OpenAI-style `/embeddings` contract with `Authorization: Bearer ...` and `data[0].embedding` responses. The Windows AI service instead exposes `POST /embed`, requires `X-API-Key`, accepts `{"text": ...}`, and returns `{"embedding": ...}`.
issues.impact: Pointing `EMBEDDING_API_BASE` at the documented canonical Windows AI service still breaks the shared embedding path before dimensionality is even relevant. Remote embedding requests and warmup probes will hit the wrong route with the wrong auth header and expect the wrong response envelope, so the canonical service cannot satisfy the repo’s main remote-embedding client contract.
issues.notes: Canonical Windows AI embedding endpoint still does not match the shared remote-embedding contract

issues.title: Qwen-VL native-vision readiness still probes with the wrong auth header
issues.summary: The shared native-vision probe always sends `X-API-Key`, but the live Qwen-VL OCR client talks to its remote runtime with `Authorization: Bearer ...`. The repo still classifies Qwen-VL under the native external vision runtime family, so a correctly configured bearer-auth Qwen-VL endpoint can fail readiness probing even though the actual OCR path would authenticate successfully.
issues.impact: Ops/readiness for Qwen-VL can still report the native vision lane unhealthy or unreachable purely because the probe uses the wrong auth scheme. That makes deployment diagnostics disagree with the actual worker OCR client and weakens the repo’s claimed shared native-vision contract.
issues.notes: Qwen-VL native-vision readiness still probes with the wrong auth header

issues.title: Qwen-VL native-vision readiness still probes the wrong endpoints for its declared transport
issues.summary: `build_native_vision_runtime()` declares Qwen-VL uses `transport="openai_vision"`, and the live Qwen-VL OCR client only talks to `/chat/completions` or `/v1/chat/completions`. But the shared native-vision readiness helper ignores `transport` entirely and only probes `/vision/runtime` and `/healthz`, which are Surya-style service endpoints rather than the actual Qwen-VL request surface.
issues.impact: A perfectly valid OpenAI-compatible Qwen-VL runtime can still fail the repo’s native-vision readiness check simply because it does not expose Surya-specific `/vision/runtime` or `/healthz` routes. That makes Qwen-VL readiness false-red by transport design, not just by configuration mistakes.
issues.notes: Qwen-VL native-vision readiness still probes the wrong endpoints for its declared transport

issues.title: Scoped artifact soft-delete still writes a non-canonical `CANCELLED` job state
issues.summary: The canonical artifact soft-delete helper now updates active jobs to the raw string `"CANCELLED"`, but the repo’s `JobStatus` enum and valid-transition table do not define that lifecycle state anywhere.
issues.impact: Soft-deleted jobs fall outside the repo’s canonical job-state machine. Any code that reasons about active/terminal jobs through `JobStatus` or the shared status helpers can silently miss these rows, and the delete path can create state that the rest of the runtime never formally recognizes.

issues.title: KB review still allows cross-case canonical binding by raw `canonical_entity_id`
issues.summary: The KB review routes still accept an arbitrary `canonical_entity_id`, and `_materialize_candidate()` resolves that canonical entity by raw UUID only. It never verifies that the target canonical entity belongs to the same case as the reviewed candidate.
issues.impact: Even after the list/update surfaces gained more scope handling, an operator who knows a canonical entity UUID can still approve a candidate in one case and attach it to a canonical entity from another case. That is still a live cross-case mutation path in the curated KB identity layer.

issues.title: Legacy explorer delete compatibility wrapper is no longer idempotent and changed its response shape
issues.summary: The legacy explorer delete route now delegates into the canonical scoped delete helper. That helper treats already-deleted artifacts as not found and returns `target_id`/`job_id` instead of the legacy explorer payload fields such as `artifact_id` and `already_deleted`.
issues.impact: Older explorer callers can now regress in two ways: repeated soft-delete requests stop being idempotent and begin returning 404, and clients expecting the legacy explorer response contract can no longer rely on `artifact_id`/`already_deleted` semantics. The security fix closed the old destructive path, but it also changed the mounted compatibility behavior.
issues.notes: Legacy explorer delete compatibility wrapper is no longer idempotent and changed its response shape

issues.title: KB review materialization still drops the submitted review notes on first approval
issues.summary: `_materialize_candidate()` copies `candidate.review_notes` into the new canonical entity description before `apply_candidate_review()` writes the incoming `notes` onto the candidate. On first approval, the newly created canonical entity therefore receives stale or empty analyst rationale instead of the review note that triggered the approval.
issues.impact: The first canonical entity created from review can lose the analyst’s explanatory note even though the review action submitted one. That weakens the curated KB audit trail and makes newly materialized canonical entities less explainable than the candidate review event that created them.
issues.notes: KB review materialization still drops the submitted review notes on first approval

issues.title: Core worker jobs still mutate `triage_result` in place and can report success without persisting refreshed metadata
issues.summary: Several live worker paths still take the existing `artifact.triage_result` dict, mutate it in place, and assign the same object back to a plain `JSONB` column. That pattern is already known to be non-dirty in this repo, but it still appears in `TEXT_EXTRACT`, `enrich_artifact()`, and `CONTENT_CLASSIFY`.
issues.impact: Successful text extraction, enrichment, and classification jobs can still leave stale `has_text_layer*`, `lang`, `content_simhash`, and `doc_classification` metadata in the database. Downstream search, similarity, stats, and classification surfaces then read old triage JSON even though the worker just finished `DONE`.
issues.notes: Core worker jobs still mutate `triage_result` in place and can report success without persisting refreshed metadata

issues.title: Explorer still populates its bucket selector from the global bucket list instead of the active case scope
issues.summary: The mounted Explorer page loads buckets once via the global `getBuckets()` helper and renders every returned bucket into the scope selector. The backend list route is still deployment-global, and the `Bucket` model itself still has no case ownership.
issues.impact: Even when an analyst is browsing a specific case, the Explorer bucket dropdown still advertises buckets from other cases and other workflows as if they were valid local scope. That makes the mounted Explorer scope UI wider than the case context it sits inside and can drive misleading empty or cross-case bucket selections.
issues.notes: Explorer still populates its bucket selector from the global bucket list instead of the active case scope

issues.title: Snapshot compare still drops detailed asset and hypothesis drift even though snapshots capture both
issues.summary: `SnapshotEngine` captures both `assets` and `hypotheses` into `snapshot_data`, and the compare route exposes the returned diff directly, but `_compute_detailed_diff()` only returns actor, event, and claim details.
issues.impact: `/cim/snapshots/compare` can report that asset counts changed while offering no per-asset detail, and it suppresses hypothesis drift entirely despite snapshots storing it. Analysts therefore get an incomplete compare result from a route that appears to be a full semantic snapshot diff.
issues.notes: Snapshot compare still drops detailed asset and hypothesis drift even though snapshots capture both

issues.title: `ANALYST_REPORT` still hard-fails when any selected artifact resolves to no text
issues.summary: `_generate_analyst_report()` stores `resolved.text` into `doc_text` and slices `doc_text[:2000]` without guarding `None`. The shared LLM-context helper already treats missing text as `""`, so the current report worker is stricter than its own helper layer and aborts the whole report instead of degrading one artifact to empty context.
issues.impact: A multi-artifact analyst report can still fail completely if even one selected artifact is image-only, binary, or otherwise unresolved to text. The job ends `FAILED` instead of producing a degraded report for the rest of the selected evidence.

issues.title: Report Studio still submits stale hidden artifact selections after the upstream selection is cleared
issues.summary: The `contextSelection` state is only refreshed when `selectedArtifacts.length > 0`. When the parent clears the selection, `contextSelection` is left untouched, the visible context manager disappears, and `runAnalysis()` still submits the stale hidden IDs and keeps `scope: "selection"`.
issues.impact: An analyst can clear the current artifact selection or move to a scope with no explicit selection and still generate a report against the previous hidden artifact set. The mounted Report Studio therefore can submit evidence the UI is no longer showing.
issues.notes: Report Studio still submits stale hidden artifact selections after the upstream selection is cleared

issues.title: Report Studio still leaves the previous scope’s active report visible after scope changes or loss of context
issues.summary: `fetchHistory()` updates only the `reports` list, and the scope effect simply skips fetching when `systemId` is absent. Nothing clears `activeReport` or `editorContent` when case/system scope changes, and polling failures are only logged, so the panel can keep rendering the previous scope’s report body after the current scope has changed or become invalid.
issues.impact: Switching systems, switching cases, or clearing artifact context can still leave old report content on screen while the panel warns that system context is missing or while history belongs to a different scope. That makes the mounted report surface capable of showing stale cross-scope report content as if it were current.
issues.notes: Report Studio still leaves the previous scope’s active report visible after scope changes or loss of context

issues.title: Roadmap docs no longer agree with the live `FACE_DETECT` queue contract
issues.summary: The runtime routes `FACE_DETECT` to `q.ocr` and the OCR worker lane, but roadmap docs still describe the same live job on `q.analysis` in O2 planning and on `q.triage` in the gap-analysis roadmap, even while agent guidance says queue routing is centralized in `common/job_contracts.py`.
issues.impact: The repo no longer has a trustworthy documented source of truth for a live queue contract. Operators and future changes can provision, inspect, or reason about the wrong worker lane for `FACE_DETECT`, which undermines the roadmap claim that queue routing is already centralized.
issues.notes: Roadmap docs no longer agree with the live `FACE_DETECT` queue contract

issues.title: Contract validation still lets unmapped job types fall through to `q.ingest`
issues.summary: `validate_job_type()` accepts any job type present in `JOB_CONTRACTS`, but `get_queue_for_job_type()` silently falls back to the literal `"q.ingest"` for unmapped jobs. `JobBuilder.enqueue()` trusts that helper, so a contract can be treated as valid and still be routed to ingest instead of failing fast, even though `JobPublisher.create_job()` would otherwise reject an unknown queue mapping.
issues.impact: A new or partially wired contract can be accepted as “known” yet silently land on the ingest queue instead of raising a routing error. That turns missing queue topology into misexecution rather than an obvious startup or publish failure.
issues.notes: Contract validation still lets unmapped job types fall through to `q.ingest`

issues.title: Ops queue controls and reliability telemetry still ignore queue-name overrides
issues.summary: The enqueue path accepts an explicit `queue_name`, and the outbox publisher persists whatever queue name it is given. But RabbitMQ depth sampling, queue drift reporting, and broker purge controls all operate on a fixed built-in queue list. Any job sent to a nonstandard queue therefore becomes invisible to the repo’s own ops and reliability surfaces.
issues.impact: A queue override or future queue family can successfully carry work while `/ops` depth, drift, and pause/purge tooling all report a clean system. That leaves incident response blind to exactly the jobs that escaped the hardcoded topology.
issues.notes: Ops queue controls and reliability telemetry still ignore queue-name overrides

issues.title: DLQ requeue still cannot actually filter by the original queue name it documents
issues.summary: `/ops/dlq/requeue` claims it can filter by the original queue name “if stuck in headers,” but live publishers never store the original queue name in message headers or body. The endpoint then ignores broker metadata and compares `queue_filter` against `_job_queue_name(job)`, which is the current contract-derived queue, not the queue the failed message originally traversed.
issues.impact: Operators cannot reliably target DLQ messages by the queue they actually failed on. After routing changes, the endpoint can filter and requeue against the new contract lane instead of the original failed lane, which makes DLQ recovery inaccurate and weakens the repo’s claimed operational guardrails.
issues.notes: DLQ requeue still cannot actually filter by the original queue name it documents

issues.title: Filtered DLQ requeue still counts preserved non-matching messages against the requested limit
issues.summary: The DLQ loop increments `processed` before it checks `queue_filter`, and filtered-out messages are merely preserved back onto the DLQ. That means `count` limits how many DLQ messages are scanned, not how many matching messages are actually requeued.
issues.impact: A request like “requeue 10 messages for queue X” can return with fewer or even zero requeues just because unrelated DLQ messages were ahead of the matches. Matching messages stay stranded deeper in the DLQ while the endpoint reports a completed run.
issues.notes: Filtered DLQ requeue still counts preserved non-matching messages against the requested limit

issues.title: Bulk pause still mutates job state without emitting any `JobEvent`
issues.summary: `pause_queued_jobs()` bulk-deletes pending outbox rows and updates jobs to `PAUSED`, but it never records any `JobEvent` entries for the pause action. The paired resume path does emit `JOB_RESUMED`, so job history can show a resume with no corresponding pause event.
issues.impact: An operator can bulk-pause queued work and erase its pending outbox deliveries without leaving any event-level audit trail on the affected jobs. That breaks evidence-first provenance for a live control-plane action and makes later “why did this job stop or resume?” investigations incomplete.
issues.notes: Bulk pause still mutates job state without emitting any `JobEvent`

issues.title: `/api/ops/check` still reports system health without checking RabbitMQ at all
issues.summary: `system_check()` is documented as a detailed system health check and even comments that database and RabbitMQ should be checked, but the implementation only reads disk usage and FTS health. Its final `"online"` status is driven solely by `fts.ready`.
issues.impact: Broker outages, queue-topology failures, or worker-delivery incidents can leave `/api/ops/check` reporting `"online"` as long as disk and FTS look healthy. That makes the repo’s advertised ops-health surface false-green on a core runtime dependency.
issues.notes: `/api/ops/check` still reports system health without checking RabbitMQ at all

issues.title: Invalid runtime-flags files can still take down core ops surfaces on first read
issues.summary: `_load_runtime_flags_or_http_error()` converts `get_all_flags()` `ValueError`s into HTTP 500s. `_load_flags()` raises that `ValueError` on a malformed `ops_flags.json` when there is no cached good copy yet. Both `system_check()` and `ops_monitor()` call the helper unconditionally, and `system_check()` does so even though it does not return any flag payload.
issues.impact: A malformed or partially written runtime-flags file can make the main ops monitor and system-check endpoints fail with 500 exactly during a configuration incident. That turns a degraded-but-diagnosable state into a control-plane outage.
issues.notes: Invalid runtime-flags files can still take down core ops surfaces on first read

issues.title: `pipeline_metrics` still marks recovered queued jobs as stale by `created_at` alone
issues.summary: The pipeline-metrics endpoint counts stale queued jobs using only `Job.created_at < stale_cutoff`, while the canonical reliability snapshot uses `coalesce(Job.updated_at, Job.created_at)`. Requeued or recently touched old jobs therefore remain flagged stale in pipeline metrics even after recovery updated them.
issues.impact: Ops can keep reporting stale-queued backlog for jobs that were just recovered, republished, or otherwise touched, which makes one control-plane metric disagree with the repo’s canonical reliability snapshot and creates false stale-job alarms.
issues.notes: `pipeline_metrics` still marks recovered queued jobs as stale by `created_at` alone

issues.title: Scoped ops views still drop artifactless report/system jobs entirely
issues.summary: Outside unrestricted global scope, `_ops_scoped_job_query()` filters out all jobs with `artifact_id IS NULL`, and `_ops_scoped_event_query()` inner-joins events through `Artifact`. But `REPORT_GENERATE` jobs are created with `artifact_id=None` even though the backing `Report` row carries `case_id` and `system_id`.
issues.impact: Case- or bucket-scoped ops dashboards, job lists, and event logs can completely miss live report-generation and other artifactless system jobs even when those jobs belong to the selected case/system. That leaves scoped operations blind to an entire class of real work.
issues.notes: Scoped ops views still drop artifactless report/system jobs entirely

issues.title: Standardized `JobEvent` metadata still loses report identity and scope for artifactless jobs
issues.summary: `build_event_metadata()` only resolves scope through `job.artifact_id`, and it does not surface the effective payload itself. For artifactless jobs like `REPORT_GENERATE`, the only durable identifier in payload is `report_id`, but the emitted metadata contains neither `report_id` nor `case_id/system_id`.
issues.impact: `RUNNING`/`DONE`/`FAILED` events for report jobs cannot be reliably tied back to the report row or its case/system scope from standardized metadata alone. That weakens the repo’s evidence-first provenance contract on a live analyst-facing pipeline.
issues.notes: Standardized `JobEvent` metadata still loses report identity and scope for artifactless jobs

issues.title: Some artifactless/system jobs are still enqueued without any `JOB_CREATED` audit event
issues.summary: The outbox layer has a dedicated `create_job_with_event()` path that emits `JOB_CREATED`, but both report generation and ops-triggered `SIMHASH_REBUILD` still call bare `create_job()`. Those jobs therefore exist in `jobs` and `job_outbox` without an initial `JobEvent` creation record.
issues.impact: If broker delivery stalls, a worker never claims the job, or a job is inspected before dispatch starts, there is no creation-time audit record explaining when or why that system job was queued. That makes artifactless control-plane work less traceable than the repo claims.
issues.notes: Some artifactless/system jobs are still enqueued without any `JOB_CREATED` audit event

issues.title: Recovery outbox rebuild rewrites jobs onto the current contract queue
issues.summary: `JobPublisher.create_job()` persists the actual queue into `JobOutbox.queue_name`, but `_ensure_outbox_entry()` recreates pending outbox rows from `get_queue_for_job_type(job.job_type)` instead. `Job` itself does not store any queue name, so once the original pending outbox row is gone recovery has no way to preserve explicit queue overrides or historical queue routing.
issues.impact: A recovered unpublished or stale queued job can be republished to a different queue than the one it was originally created for. Queue overrides and historical routing changes therefore turn recovery into silent misrouting instead of faithful replay.
issues.notes: Recovery outbox rebuild rewrites jobs onto the current contract queue

issues.title: API startup never enforces the startup guardrails the roadmap says exist
issues.summary: `enforce_startup_guard()` is implemented and the worker runs it before warming models or consuming jobs, but the API lifespan never calls that guard at all. The API only runs schema/FTS/migration startup tasks, and those are controlled by separate `API_RUN_STARTUP_TASKS` / `API_STARTUP_STRICT` flags. Roadmap docs still describe startup guardrails as a live code surface.
issues.impact: The worker can fail fast on restore-collapse conditions while the API still boots and serves requests against the same bad corpus state. In practice the startup guard is worker-only, so the control plane can come up “normally” even when the repo’s own guardrail should have blocked runtime startup.
issues.notes: API startup never enforces the startup guardrails the roadmap says exist

issues.title: `/api/health` still reports `ok` while runtime reliability is already degraded
issues.summary: `/api/health` computes DB reliability metrics including `stale_queued`, `stale_running`, `outbox_pending`, and `outbox_exhausted`, but its top-level `status` only depends on DB connectivity and RabbitMQ connectivity. It does not let degraded reliability, schema failure, or skipped startup tasks affect the reported health state even though the O2 guardrails say health endpoints must report degraded state honestly.
issues.impact: Operators and automation can see a green `/api/health` response while the runtime is already accumulating stale queued jobs or exhausted outbox entries. That makes the “deep” health endpoint false-green on real delivery and recovery failures rather than just on missing process liveness.
issues.notes: `/api/health` still reports `ok` while runtime reliability is already degraded

issues.title: Enabled startup guardrails are still a warning-only no-op under default settings
issues.summary: The startup guard is enabled by default, but its default thresholds configure no required checks: `STARTUP_GUARD_MIN_ARTIFACTS=0`, `STARTUP_GUARD_REQUIRE_CHECKS=False`, and if the manifest file is absent the guard only logs “Startup guard is enabled but no checks are configured” before returning `passed=True`.
issues.impact: Even the runtime that does call `enforce_startup_guard()` can still boot with no effective restore-collapse checks configured. The repo therefore advertises startup guardrails as present while the default behavior is effectively “warn and continue.”
issues.notes: Enabled startup guardrails are still a warning-only no-op under default settings

issues.title: Live recovery defaults still do not heal published-but-lost queue drift
issues.summary: `recover_stale_queued_jobs()` has a dedicated `include_published=True` mode for “queue drift healing after broker resets,” but the worker startup path, the periodic worker stale check, and `run_all_recovery_routines()` all call it with the default `False`. Even the ops reconcile endpoint defaults `include_published_stale` to `False` while documenting itself as reconciling “queue drift by forcing DB→outbox delivery truth.”
issues.impact: Jobs whose broker messages were lost after `published_at` was already set remain stranded in normal runtime operation unless an operator knows to opt into the special published-stale mode. The queue-drift healing path exists in code, but the live defaults still leave that failure mode untouched.
issues.notes: Live recovery defaults still do not heal published-but-lost queue drift

issues.title: `/api/health` ignores the FTS invariant that API startup treats as fatal
issues.summary: API startup calls `require_fts_invariant()` and can fail strict startup when the `chunks` FTS trigger/index invariant is missing, but `/api/health` never runs that check or even `get_fts_health_snapshot()`. The route only reports schema status plus DB/RabbitMQ connectivity and can therefore stay green while the search invariant startup is meant to protect is broken.
issues.impact: The “deep” API health surface can report healthy even when the runtime search/indexing invariant that startup is supposed to enforce is missing. That creates another false-green control-plane path around one of the repo’s core data-plane guarantees.
issues.notes: `/api/health` ignores the FTS invariant that API startup treats as fatal

issues.title: Translation failures are still persisted as if segments were successfully translated
issues.summary: Multiple live translation backends still return the original source text on hard failure or unsupported-source paths instead of surfacing a failed segment. `translation_flow` then strips that value, appends it to `translated_segments`, and hashes it into segment provenance without checking the backend status, so untranslated source text can be committed as the "translated" output.
issues.impact: A runtime translation exception can silently degrade into "output equals input" while the pipeline still stores provenance hashes and downstream artifacts as if translation succeeded. That corrupts analyst trust, variant truth, and any downstream workflows that treat translated text as a distinct derived asset.

issues.title: Worker bootstrap still treats outbox flusher startup failure as log-only
issues.summary: The worker bootstrap unconditionally calls `_start_outbox_flusher()`, but `_start_outbox_flusher()` catches all exceptions and only logs them before continuing startup. `JobPublisher.create_job()` and chained-job creation still implement the outbox pattern by writing `Job` plus `JobOutbox` rows, so a worker can come up and process broker work even though its own follow-on publishing path never started.
issues.impact: A broken flusher thread leaves newly created jobs, chained jobs, and recovery republish work stranded in the DB outbox while the worker still looks "up". This is a core delivery failure that degrades into partial runtime behavior instead of a hard startup failure.

issues.title: ODF preview and projection paths still iterate a dead `_children` object model and drop document content
issues.summary: `common/ooxml/xml_parser.py` now parses XML into real `ElementTree` elements, but the ODF parser still traverses nodes as dict-like objects with `_children` and `_tag`. The ODT, ODS, and ODP entry points therefore walk empty attribute defaults instead of actual child elements, while preview and OOXML projection still route `.odt`, `.ods`, and `.odp` through those broken functions.
issues.impact: Live ODF preview/projection surfaces can produce empty or severely incomplete output even when the files are valid. That breaks text extraction and preview fidelity for an entire supported document family rather than a corner-case transform.

issues.title: Fused-claim promotion scores stay non-zero even when there is no supporting evidence
issues.summary: `derive_fused_claim_status()` correctly leaves claims without positive support in `external_context`, but `compute_promotion_score()` still clamps source quality, source independence, support strength, and retrieval/grounding quality to a minimum of `0.2`. As a result, rows with zero positive evidence but non-zero resolve confidence still get a non-zero promotion score, and the fused-knowledge service persists that score directly into `FusedClaim.confidence`.
issues.impact: The claim ledger can assign positive confidence to claims that have no actual supporting signals, which undermines the repo's stated evidence-first fused-knowledge discipline. Downstream graph/report consumers can therefore treat unsupported claims as partially trusted instead of clearly unpromoted context.

issues.title: `reconcile_missing_outputs()` still bypasses runtime recovery safety gates and audit events
issues.summary: The maintenance reconcile path scans DONE OCR jobs for missing outputs and directly rewrites those jobs back to `QUEUED` with a fresh outbox row. Unlike canonical recovery, it never calls `get_recovery_block_reason()` before republishing and never emits the `JobEvent` audit row that recovery writes for a requeue transition.
issues.impact: A missing derived OCR file can cause maintenance to republish jobs that canonical recovery would have blocked for deleted/quarantined/security-mismatched artifacts, and it does so without leaving the normal requeue audit trail. That creates a second, less safe recovery path for the same core lifecycle transition.
issues.notes: `reconcile_missing_outputs()` still bypasses runtime recovery safety gates and audit events

issues.title: Worker startup checks can still false-pass broken storage by creating roots and reading the wrong vault setting
issues.summary: `run_startup_checks()` treats missing storage roots as something to create during the health check itself, which converts absent or mis-mounted directories into a green result. The vault check also reads `settings.VAULT_DIR` even though the config model only defines `VAULT_ROOT`, so the check is wired to a non-existent setting name.
issues.impact: Startup validation can mark storage healthy even when the intended mount is missing, and the vault branch is not actually checking the configured vault root field. That weakens one of the few early signals that the worker is pointed at the wrong filesystem state.
issues.notes: Worker startup checks can still false-pass broken storage by creating roots and reading the wrong vault setting

issues.title: Native Surya service health still reports client-side `SURYA_API_BASE` instead of server-local runtime truth
issues.summary: The Surya service builds its advertised runtime dict from `SURYA_API_BASE` and `SURYA_API_KEY`, which are the client-facing remote-runtime settings, not the server's own local OCR availability. `get_surya_status_payload()` and `run_surya_ocr()` then mix that runtime dict with `get_surya_processor()` state, so `/healthz`, `/vision/runtime`, and OCR responses can report an empty/disabled runtime even while local Surya OCR is actually available and serving requests.
issues.impact: The canonical Surya service can self-report as misconfigured or disabled purely because the client-side `SURYA_API_BASE` env is unset, even though its local OCR path is healthy. That makes health/readiness payloads disagree with actual service behavior and creates false-red diagnostics on the repo's documented native vision endpoint.
issues.notes: Native Surya service health still reports client-side `SURYA_API_BASE` instead of server-local runtime truth

issues.title: Native Surya service still violates the class-3 external-boundary contract by loading worker OCR internals in-process
issues.summary: `services/native_vision/surya_service.py` imports `worker.src.processors.ocr.surya.get_surya_processor()` and uses that worker-owned singleton to load and execute OCR. The runtime policy and blueprint both say class-3 native vision runtimes belong in `services/native_vision/` and must be external service boundaries rather than worker-owned in-process model loads.
issues.impact: The canonical native-vision service is not actually a clean external boundary. It inherits worker package layout, worker-only dependencies, and worker runtime behavior, which weakens the separation the repo claims for class-3 vision runtimes and makes service bring-up more brittle than the documented contract.
issues.notes: Native Surya service still violates the class-3 external-boundary contract by loading worker OCR internals in-process

issues.title: Canonical Windows AI OCR service still false-greens CPU-unavailable hosts
issues.summary: The Windows AI health route always returns `"status": "online"` and separately reports `gpu=torch.cuda.is_available()`, but the OCR loader still hard-codes `PaddleOCR(... use_gpu=True ...)` with no CPU fallback. Unlike the translator and embedder loaders, the OCR path never switches behavior when CUDA is unavailable, so a CPU-only or broken-GPU host can look alive while OCR requests fail on first use.
issues.impact: Operators can see the canonical Windows AI endpoint as online even though its OCR lane is guaranteed to fail without a working CUDA path. That turns the service health surface into a false-green for one of the endpoint's primary capabilities rather than a real readiness check.
issues.notes: Canonical Windows AI OCR service still false-greens CPU-unavailable hosts

issues.title: System-scoped report reads still cannot see queued selection reports
issues.summary: The analysis read/history helper treats `system_id` scope as `Report.system_id == system_id AND Report.case_id IS NULL`, but selection-scope report generation stores both `case_id` and `system_id` on the same `Report` row. System-only report reads and history therefore exclude selection reports even though those reports are still tied to the requested system.
issues.impact: A queued or completed selection report can disappear from system-scoped history and direct report reads unless the caller also knows and passes `case_id`. That breaks the report control plane on a core happy path, not just on a niche scope edge.

issues.title: Orchestrator still accepts `system_id` without ever enforcing it
issues.summary: `OrchestrateRequest` advertises `system_id`, but `_assert_artifact_scope()` only checks deletion, security clearance, bucket membership, and case access. The orchestration loop passes `case_id` and `bucket_id` only, so `system_id` is a dead request field with no artifact-boundary enforcement.
issues.impact: A caller can send a system-scoped orchestration request and still queue work for artifacts outside that system as long as case/bucket checks pass. The API contract therefore advertises a scope boundary that the live route does not honor.
issues.notes: Orchestrator still accepts `system_id` without ever enforcing it

issues.title: API rate limiting still fully exempts private-address clients
issues.summary: `_is_trusted_client_ip()` marks any loopback or RFC1918 address as trusted, and `RateLimitMiddleware` skips the `RATE_LIMIT` check entirely for trusted clients. In deployments where `request.client.host` is the reverse proxy or load balancer address, the rate limiter becomes a no-op for all traffic arriving through that private hop.
issues.impact: A common production topology can bypass the API's only built-in rate limiter completely. That turns the control into "trust the network path" rather than actual per-client throttling and leaves burst protection ineffective where it matters most.
issues.notes: API rate limiting still fully exempts private-address clients

issues.title: Legacy `/api/stats/queue` still exposes unscoped global runtime telemetry
issues.summary: `P2_25M03_234` restates the already logged orchestrator defect that the route accepts `system_id` but never enforces or uses it. Keep `P2_25M03_106` as the canonical entry for that issue.
issues.impact: Even after the ops/dashboard migration, a live legacy endpoint still leaks global queue and classification telemetry outside any explicit scope contract. That weakens the repo's scoped-control-plane discipline and preserves an easy stale surface for UI or tooling to bind to incorrectly.
issues.notes: Legacy `/api/stats/queue` still exposes unscoped global runtime telemetry

issues.title: `/keylogs/summary` still bypasses `ENFORCE_CASE_AUTH` and exposes global keylog aggregates
issues.summary: The keylog summary route accepts optional `case_id`, `system_id`, and `batch_id`, but when none are supplied it simply queries all `Artifact` rows for `source_area == "Keylogs"` and applies only the generic visibility filters. Unlike other scoped routers, it never checks `settings.ENFORCE_CASE_AUTH` or requires a case scope before returning aggregate apps, titles, URLs, and domains.
issues.impact: Under case-auth deployments, a caller can still fetch a global aggregate of keylog-derived applications, window titles, URLs, domains, file counts, and byte counts without any case scope at all. That is a live cross-case telemetry leak on a mounted analysis surface, not just a legacy docs mismatch.

issues.title: LLM model-availability cache is still shared across different credentials
issues.summary: `_MODEL_CACHE` is keyed only by `models_url`, but `_get_cached_models()` is called with auth headers derived from the current `settings.LLM_API_KEY`. Model availability learned under one credential therefore contaminates later model resolution for other credentials hitting the same endpoint URL.
issues.impact: Rotated keys, per-tenant credentials, or mixed privilege levels behind one LLM endpoint can silently reuse the wrong advertised model list. That can force unnecessary fallback to the wrong model or keep trying a model the current credential cannot access, turning a credential-bound contract into shared stale global state.
issues.notes: LLM model-availability cache is still shared across different credentials

issues.title: Connector execution still auto-merges external output into core `Artifact.triage_result`
issues.summary: `run_enabled_connectors_for_artifact()` writes each executed connector payload directly into `artifact.triage_result["connector_results"]`, even though ADR-0002 explicitly says connector output must not auto-merge into core tables and must first land in the derived layer. Both the worker connector job path and the mounted artifact route call the same helper.
issues.impact: Optional connector data crosses the repo's own derived-layer boundary and becomes part of canonical artifact triage state from both background and manual execution paths. That makes external integrations mutate core artifact state directly, which is exactly the guardrail the ADR says should not happen.
issues.notes: Connector execution still auto-merges external output into core `Artifact.triage_result`

issues.title: Threat rescans still create duplicate `ArtifactThreatMatch` rows
issues.summary: `ThreatMatcher.scan()` unconditionally inserts a new `ArtifactThreatMatch` row for every hit and never checks whether that `artifact_id` / `entity_id` pair already exists. The table definition also has no uniqueness constraint, and the enrich pipeline calls `scan()` on normal processing.
issues.impact: Retries, re-enrichment, or repeated scans can accumulate duplicate match rows for the same artifact/entity relationship. That inflates downstream threat counts and makes repeated processing look like new evidence instead of the same match being observed again.
issues.notes: Threat rescans still create duplicate `ArtifactThreatMatch` rows

issues.title: Translation layout selection still drops valid mixed-type blocks before translation and persistence
issues.summary: `select_layout_blocks()` chooses a single preferred block type per page and discards other valid positioned blocks from that page. Translation preparation uses this filtered set, and the translation flow later derives both overlay text and persisted translated markdown/text from the resulting layout.
issues.impact: Pages that legitimately contain a mix of `Line`, `Block`, `Paragraph`, or `Word` layout records can lose captured text before translation even begins. The resulting translated text, markdown, and overlay output can therefore omit valid page content that was present in the original layout extraction.
issues.notes: Translation layout selection still drops valid mixed-type blocks before translation and persistence

issues.title: Contract-level `allow_skip` and `skip_validation` rules are still dead metadata
issues.summary: `JobContract` defines `allow_skip`, `skip_reason`, `skip_validation`, and a dedicated `validate_skip_conditions()` helper, but the dispatcher never consults that contract skip machinery. Runtime execution only validates inputs/outputs and relies on ad hoc handler-thrown `SkipJob`s instead.
issues.impact: The repo advertises contract-driven skip semantics for jobs like `TRANSLATE`, `GENERATE_EMBEDDING`, `EVENT_BUILD`, `CONNECTOR_RUN`, `NAT_GATE`, and `CONTENT_CLASSIFY`, but the live worker ignores that layer completely. Any job path that depends on those contract skip rules still needs bespoke handler logic or it will fail/complete under the wrong lifecycle despite the contract saying a skip is valid.
issues.notes: Contract-level `allow_skip` and `skip_validation` rules are still dead metadata

issues.title: NAT gate still lets non-suspicious macro-bearing Office files pass despite `allow_macros=False`
issues.summary: ADR-0003 says the default security policy is `allow_macros=False`, where macro detection itself should quarantine the artifact. The live malware-triage path only quarantines macros when the extracted macro report is marked `suspicious` or contains an external URL, so ordinary detected macros can still flow to `PASSED`.
issues.impact: Macro-enabled Office evidence can still clear the security gate and fan out into preview/OCR/analysis as long as `oletools` finds VBA but does not label it suspicious. That is a direct policy/runtime mismatch on the core NAT gate, not just a documentation drift.

issues.title: SQL raw-text suppression still ignores the legacy preview-marker rule used by Python paths
issues.summary: Python text-layer routing treats the legacy Bangla preview marker as enough reason to distrust raw text, but `raw_text_untrusted_sql()` omits that condition. SQL-based retrieval and grounding paths therefore do not suppress raw-layer chunks for some artifacts that Python paths already force onto OCR.
issues.impact: Search, semantic retrieval, and deep-analysis grounding can still rank or surface raw text for artifacts whose preview already carries the legacy marker that Python routing uses to prefer OCR. That creates cross-surface text-layer drift and can reintroduce corrupted raw content into retrieval-driven workflows.
issues.notes: SQL raw-text suppression still ignores the legacy preview-marker rule used by Python paths

issues.title: `db:security_state` output validation still false-greens `NAT_GATE`
issues.summary: The `NAT_GATE` contract says the job produces `db:security_state`, but the shared validator only checks whether `artifact.security_state` is non-empty. Since the model field is non-null with a default of `STAGED`, output validation passes even when no terminal NAT result was produced.
issues.impact: Contract validation cannot prove that `NAT_GATE` actually advanced the artifact out of its default security state or finalized a real gate outcome. That leaves the worker able to mark NAT-gate jobs `DONE` on the strength of a pre-existing `STAGED` field rather than a meaningful security transition.
issues.notes: `db:security_state` output validation still false-greens `NAT_GATE`

issues.title: `db:triage_result` output validation still treats any old triage JSON as current-job output
issues.summary: The shared validator treats `db:triage_result` as satisfied whenever `artifact.triage_result` is merely truthy. Multiple live contracts rely on that token as their produced output, so a pre-existing triage blob from earlier pipeline stages can mask a current job's failure to write the output it claims to produce.
issues.impact: `CONNECTOR_RUN`, `IMAGE_INTEL_LIGHT`, `IMAGE_INTEL_HEAVY`, `FACE_DETECT`, and `CONTENT_CLASSIFY` can all false-green contract validation whenever the artifact already has some earlier triage data. That turns a shared output contract into “triage JSON exists somewhere” instead of proving the current transform actually wrote its advertised result.
issues.notes: `db:triage_result` output validation still treats any old triage JSON as current-job output

issues.title: The advertised centralized priority registry is still dead and drifted from live scheduling
issues.summary: `common/runtime/priorities.py` claims to be the centralized job-priority registry, but nothing imports or calls it. Live priorities are instead hardcoded in `common/orchestration_policy.py` and worker enqueue helpers, so editing the registry has no runtime effect and the values have already drifted.
issues.impact: The repo still presents a single source of truth for priorities that the runtime does not actually use. Priority changes can therefore be made in the “central” registry with zero effect, while real queue urgency keeps drifting across policy and helper call sites.
issues.notes: The advertised centralized priority registry is still dead and drifted from live scheduling

issues.title: `DEEP_ANALYSIS` output validation is still impossible to satisfy
issues.summary: The `DEEP_ANALYSIS` contract declares `db:analysis_result` as its produced output, and the dispatcher always validates contract outputs after the handler runs. But `_check_requirement()` never implements `db:analysis_result`, so the token always falls through to `False`.
issues.impact: A `DEEP_ANALYSIS` job can write `artifact.analysis_result` and still be forced into the failure path because the shared validator has no success condition for the declared output token. That makes the contract layer self-contradictory on a live core job type.

issues.title: `db:triage_done` output validation still false-greens `MALWARE_TRIAGE`
issues.summary: The `MALWARE_TRIAGE` contract declares `db:triage_done`, but the validator equates that token with any truthy `artifact.triage_result`. It does not check that malware triage actually ran successfully or that the current job produced a completed triage outcome.
issues.impact: Any pre-existing or partial `triage_result` can satisfy `MALWARE_TRIAGE` output validation. That weakens the security-gate proof point for a core security job and can let the worker treat stale triage JSON as a fresh malware-triage completion.
issues.notes: `db:triage_done` output validation still false-greens `MALWARE_TRIAGE`

issues.title: `db:cim_events` output validation still accepts unrelated stale event rows as current `EVENT_BUILD` output
issues.summary: The `EVENT_BUILD` contract uses `db:cim_events`, but the validator only checks whether any `CIMEvent` row exists for the artifact. The builder itself only manages rows with `properties.builder == "event_builder_v2"`, so older or externally-created event rows can satisfy validation even when the current builder's managed output was not produced.
issues.impact: `EVENT_BUILD` can be validated against stale or differently-produced CIM rows instead of the current event-builder output it is supposed to own. That makes the contract token too weak to prove that the builder actually refreshed the artifact's managed event graph.
issues.notes: `db:cim_events` output validation still accepts unrelated stale event rows as current `EVENT_BUILD` output

issues.title: `parse_docx_projection()` still returns bare strings on failure paths and crashes `.docx` projection callers
issues.summary: `parse_docx_projection()` is declared to return `ParsedMarkupDocument`, and the generic projection wrapper immediately dereferences `.html`, `.text`, `.markdown`, and `.rich_doc` on its return value. But several early DOCX error paths still return `""`, so malformed or internally-missing DOCX files raise `AttributeError` in `project_ooxml_bytes()` instead of degrading to an empty parsed document.
issues.impact: A `.docx` file that is merely malformed or missing internal parts can crash the projection wrapper with a type error instead of yielding an empty parsed result. That turns a recoverable parser failure into a harder failure path for DOCX preview/text projection surfaces.
issues.notes: `parse_docx_projection()` still returns bare strings on failure paths and crashes `.docx` projection callers

issues.title: ODS column definitions are still rendered as visible header rows
issues.summary: Beneath the already-logged ODF object-model breakage, the ODF renderer still misinterprets `table:table-column` metadata as visible table content. It maps each column-definition node to `<tr><th>...</th></tr>` and repeats it via `table:number-columns-repeated`, even though those nodes only declare column properties and should not emit user-visible rows.
issues.impact: Even after the broader `_children` traversal bug is fixed, ODS tables will still grow phantom empty header rows and cells before the real sheet data. That keeps spreadsheet preview/projection structurally wrong rather than merely blank.
issues.notes: ODS column definitions are still rendered as visible header rows

issues.title: ODS cells still default to `rowspan="0"` and `colspan="0"` on normal unmerged cells
issues.summary: The ODF table renderer unconditionally emits `rowspan` and `colspan` attributes for every `table:table-cell`, defaulting both to `0` when the source cell is not merged. In HTML, zero spans do not mean “no span”; they mean “span to the end of the remaining rows/columns,” so ordinary cells are rendered as spanning far more of the table than intended.
issues.impact: Even with the higher-level ODF traversal repaired, normal ODS tables will still render with corrupted structure because unmerged cells are promoted into end-spanning cells. That breaks table fidelity, row/column alignment, and any downstream text extraction derived from the rendered HTML grid.
issues.notes: ODS cells still default to `rowspan="0"` and `colspan="0"` on normal unmerged cells

issues.title: Successful `TRANSLATE` persistence still crashes in translation-lineage hashing
issues.summary: The translation flow defines `_sha256_path()` with `hashlib.sha256(...)` but never imports `hashlib` at module scope. Successful persistence always routes through `_write_translation_variant_outputs()` and `_persist_translation_lineage_rows()` when `job_id` is present, and the live OCR runtime passes `ctx.job.job_id` into `process_translate_advanced()`, so completed translation jobs hit a `NameError` while writing lineage hashes.
issues.impact: A live `TRANSLATE` job can finish the expensive translation work and still fail during persistence before lineage rows are written. That turns successful output into a hard job failure and blocks the same lineage-complete state the translation freshness checks later require.

issues.title: LLM context still ignores `max_chunks` and collapses DB fallback to a single chunk
issues.summary: `resolve_artifact_context()` and `read_artifact_context()` advertise a `max_chunks` parameter, and core analysis/report callers pass `max_chunks=5`, but the value is never forwarded into `resolve_artifact_text()`. The DB fallback in `resolve_artifact_text()` only looks at the preferred chunk indices and returns the first matching row, so chunk-backed grounding is hard-capped to one chunk regardless of the requested budget.
issues.impact: Deep-analysis and analyst-report paths believe they are grounding on up to five chunks, but chunk-only artifacts actually contribute at most one fallback chunk. That silently reduces coverage of long artifacts whenever derived text files are missing or stale.
issues.notes: LLM context still ignores `max_chunks` and collapses DB fallback to a single chunk

issues.title: Surya OCR readiness still ignores runtime-flag overrides in the cached model snapshot
issues.summary: `get_model_readiness_snapshot()` loads runtime flags, but `_build_base_readiness_libraries()` still computes `surya_ocr` readiness by calling `_probe_surya_readiness({}, probe_load=False)` with an empty flags dict. `/ops/ocr/providers` then combines that cached readiness object with a separate flag-based status field, so readiness can reflect static settings while status reflects the live runtime override.
issues.impact: The OCR control plane can show Surya as disabled while still surfacing readiness derived from `settings.ENABLE_SURYA_OCR`, or the reverse after flag changes. That makes `/ops/ocr/providers` unreliable for diagnosing the live OCR backend state the worker is actually honoring.
issues.notes: Surya OCR readiness still ignores runtime-flag overrides in the cached model snapshot

issues.title: Qwen-VL OCR is still live in runtime but absent from the ops/readiness control plane
issues.summary: The runtime has a real `qwen_vl` OCR backend behind config and runtime flags, and the structured OCR router can prioritize or fall back to it. But `/ops/ocr/providers` only reports `tesseract` and `surya`, and the readiness snapshot only exposes `surya_ocr` for OCR, leaving the control plane blind to a backend the worker can actively use.
issues.impact: When Qwen-VL OCR is enabled, the worker can route documents through a backend that the ops UI and readiness consumers never display. That hides real backend selection and leaves operators without any control-plane visibility into one of the live OCR paths.
issues.notes: Qwen-VL OCR is still live in runtime but absent from the ops/readiness control plane

issues.title: Quick-to-structured OCR escalation is still self-canceling
issues.summary: `OCR_QUICK` can enqueue `OCR_STRUCTURED` when tables, low confidence, or gibberish signals are detected. But the shared OCR entry path always runs `_should_skip_existing_ocr()` before it even derives the job-specific router request, and that skip gate only checks for generic OCR outputs (`layout.json`, `ocr.txt`, chunk 1). As a result, the follow-on `OCR_STRUCTURED` job sees the quick-OCR outputs and exits immediately instead of executing the structured pass it was escalated for.
issues.impact: The system can detect that quick OCR quality is insufficient, mark the artifact as escalated, and still never run the structured OCR job meant to fix it. On `run_translate_after` flows, the quick job also returns early once escalation is marked, so the intended OCR upgrade can be silently replaced by “do nothing further.”

issues.title: Mounted-path ingest still bypasses the registration/version provenance model
issues.summary: The mounted-path intake endpoint stages files and queues `IMPORT_FILE` worker jobs, but the worker import path only creates or reuses `Artifact` rows. It never creates `ArtifactRegistration` or `ArtifactVersion` rows, even though the durable provenance model explicitly represents each artifact appearance by batch/source/path and the direct-upload intake path uses that model.
issues.impact: Mounted-path ingest can persist artifacts that never acquire the batch/path registration and version lineage the rest of the product expects. That leaves registration-aware provenance, lineage, and scope surfaces incomplete specifically for the mounted worker-import path.

issues.title: Same-case duplicate mounted imports still suppress all initial ingest fan-out
issues.summary: When the worker import path finds the same SHA already present in the case, it sets `skipped_existing_case=True`, logs an informational duplicate event, and then skips `enqueue_initial_import_jobs()` entirely. Re-importing the file into the same case therefore does not requeue triage or indexing even if the reused artifact is missing outputs or needs healing.
issues.impact: Mounted re-import is not a healing path for same-case artifacts. Once a document hash already exists in the case, rerunning import cannot repair missing triage/index state because the worker refuses to fan the artifact back into the normal ingest pipeline.
issues.notes: Same-case duplicate mounted imports still suppress all initial ingest fan-out

issues.title: Job dedupe still fails open on DB/query errors and silently enqueues duplicates
issues.summary: `has_existing_job()` catches any exception from the dedupe query, rolls the session back, and returns `(None, safe_fallback)`. With the default `safe_fallback=False`, callers interpret the result as “no existing job,” so a broken dedupe read falls through into new job creation instead of surfacing an error.
issues.impact: A transient SQL/session failure in the dedupe read path is treated as proof that no active job exists, so the system can enqueue duplicate `TRANSLATE`, `EVENT_BUILD`, and other follow-on jobs exactly when the DB is least healthy. That turns a defensive dedupe layer into a fail-open duplication path.
issues.notes: Job dedupe still fails open on DB/query errors and silently enqueues duplicates

issues.title: Dedupe payload merges still commit through `auto_commit=False` transaction boundaries
issues.summary: `merge_job_payload_hints()` unconditionally calls `db.commit()` whenever it updates an existing deduped job payload. `enqueue_follow_on_dedup()` uses that helper on the already-exists path even when the caller explicitly passed `auto_commit=False`, so a dedupe hit can commit unrelated surrounding job/artifact work early.
issues.impact: A dedupe hit no longer preserves the caller’s transaction boundary. Worker paths that asked not to auto-commit can still persist partial state early just because an existing queued job had its payload upgraded, which weakens atomicity around job chaining and artifact state updates.
issues.notes: Dedupe payload merges still commit through `auto_commit=False` transaction boundaries

issues.title: Process tracking still merges unrelated cases into the same thread by name-only lookup
issues.summary: The process-tracking processor derives a `ProcessThread` name either from `case.name` or from a global daily auto-name, then looks up existing threads by `ProcessThread.name` alone. It does not scope that lookup by `case_id`, so same-named cases or same-day auto threads can absorb each other’s events into a shared thread.
issues.impact: Process-thread narratives can cross-contaminate across cases or across unrelated artifacts processed on the same day. That undermines the evidentiary integrity of `ProcessThread` / `ProcessEvent` timelines by letting scope collisions rewrite the same operational thread.
issues.notes: Process tracking still merges unrelated cases into the same thread by name-only lookup

issues.title: Scoped thread reads still break when bundle scope is only derivable from member artifacts
issues.summary: `threads.py` already carries `_bundle_scope_signature()` to recover `case_id` / `system_id` from `AnalysisBundleItem -> Artifact -> Batch` when bundle columns are null, and the merge route relies on that fallback. But `list_threads()` and `get_thread_timeline()` still filter and authorize only against `AnalysisBundle.case_id` / `AnalysisBundle.system_id`. Historical or partially backfilled thread bundles with null scope columns therefore disappear from scoped listings and 404 on scoped timeline reads even when every member artifact belongs to the requested scope.
issues.impact: Scoped analysts can lose access to valid legacy thread bundles: they will not show up in `/threads?case_id=...` / `/threads?system_id=...`, and direct timeline reads can return `404 Thread not found` even though the bundle’s member artifacts are entirely inside the requested scope.
issues.notes: Scoped thread reads still break when bundle scope is only derivable from member artifacts

issues.title: API main app still leaks raw internal exception text to clients
issues.summary: The global exception handler formats unhandled exceptions straight into the JSON response body, and the plain `/health` / `/health/ready` probes also include raw database exception text in their `detail` strings. Those endpoints therefore expose internal runtime error messages directly to callers instead of returning a generic failure response.
issues.impact: Clients can receive backend exception text such as database connection failures, driver messages, file paths, or other runtime internals. That widens information disclosure during ordinary failures and makes production errors easier to fingerprint from outside the service.
issues.notes: API main app still leaks raw internal exception text to clients

issues.title: Registration-aware case reuse is still invisible to `find_case_artifact_by_sha()`
issues.summary: `find_case_artifact_by_sha()` says it finds artifacts already registered under a case, but it only joins `Artifact -> Batch` and filters on `Batch.case_id`. The registration flow writes case-scoped ownership into `ArtifactRegistration` and `ArtifactVersion` without changing `Artifact.batch_id`, and upload paths reuse cross-case canonicals through that registration layer. Once a canonical artifact is reused into a second case, later case-scoped hash checks still miss it and some intake surfaces misclassify it as cross-case only.
issues.impact: After a file is reused into a second case through the registration-aware upload path, `/intake/hash-seen` can still report `exact_case_exists=false`, and case-scoped register-existing flows can throw the `"Cross-case register-existing is disabled"` conflict even though the artifact is already registered in that same case. That makes the new provenance model internally inconsistent on a core intake/read path.
issues.notes: Registration-aware case reuse is still invisible to `find_case_artifact_by_sha()`

issues.title: `text_chunking` still emits over-target chunks for single long tokens
issues.summary: `_split_overlong_fragment()` recursively re-splits oversized sentence fragments, but its word-level fallback never handles a single word that is itself longer than `target_chars`. A blob with no useful sentence breaks, such as a huge URL, hash string, or encoded token, can therefore come back as one chunk far above the configured target size.
issues.impact: Retrieval chunk writes and remote embedding requests can still receive pathological multi-kilobyte chunks even when the system is configured for much smaller boundaries. That increases the chance of poor retrieval granularity, oversized provider payloads, and avoidable failures on documents containing long unbroken tokens.
issues.notes: `text_chunking` still emits over-target chunks for single long tokens

issues.title: Source-area baseline classification hints still never raise the persisted level
issues.summary: The classifier defines minimum baseline sensitivity hints by source area and document type, including `KEYLOGS -> RESTRICTED`. But after computing `baseline_level`, the implementation only floors the confidence score and never upgrades the returned `ClassificationResult.level`. The worker then persists that unchanged `result.level` directly onto the artifact.
issues.impact: Baseline-sensitive source areas can still be stored as `UNCLASSIFIED` or another lower detected level even when classifier metadata says the minimum should be higher. That makes the baseline policy operationally inert for Explorer, overrides, and downstream audit surfaces that rely on the persisted classification level itself.
issues.notes: Source-area baseline classification hints still never raise the persisted level

issues.title: Triage-result schema migration still silently skips missing intermediate migrators
issues.summary: `migrate_triage_result()` increments `schema_version` on every loop iteration whether or not a migrator exists for that version. If a future intermediate migration is absent from `_MIGRATIONS`, the function still advances the payload to the final version number and returns it as if the missing transform had run.
issues.impact: A missing future migration becomes a silent false-green instead of a hard failure. Older `triage_result` payloads can be stamped with the current schema version while still missing the transforms required to make their shape compatible with live readers.
issues.notes: Triage-result schema migration still silently skips missing intermediate migrators

issues.title: PPTX paragraph rendering still drops all content after the first inline drawing
issues.summary: `process_paragraph()` enters a loop over inline drawings, but both the lazy-placeholder path and the extracted-image path `return` immediately from the whole function. Any remaining inline drawings and every subsequent text run in that paragraph are therefore skipped whenever the first drawing is successfully processed.
issues.impact: PPTX paragraphs that contain inline drawings can lose the rest of their content in HTML preview and projection output. Analysts may see only the first embedded image or placeholder while the paragraph’s surrounding text and later drawings disappear entirely.
issues.notes: PPTX paragraph rendering still drops all content after the first inline drawing

issues.title: Remote Surya OCR failures are still persisted as successful empty OCR output
issues.summary: The remote Surya client returns `{}` after request failures instead of raising. The structured OCR router also returns `{}` after all Surya processors fail, and the OCR flow then treats that empty payload as “no text extracted” and persists an empty OCR result instead of failing the job.
issues.impact: A Surya outage, auth failure, or malformed remote response can become a `DONE` OCR job with empty `ocr.txt`, empty `layout.json`, deleted OCR chunks, and `triage_result.ocr.status="empty"`. That hides a real backend failure as artifact-level “no OCR text” and can strand documents in a misleading healed-looking state.
issues.notes: Remote Surya OCR failures are still persisted as successful empty OCR output

issues.title: `run_analysis_with_fallback()` still has no fallback and fails `ANALYST_REPORT` outright
issues.summary: The only path inside `run_analysis_with_fallback()` is a single external LLM request. On any LLM error it raises immediately, despite the exported interface promising fallback behavior. `ReportProcessor` propagates that exception into a failed report.
issues.impact: `ANALYST_REPORT` has no degraded-output or alternate-engine path. Any external LLM outage or transient failure turns the whole report into `FAILED` instead of producing a fallback report body or partial result, despite the function name and callsite implying resilience.
issues.notes: `run_analysis_with_fallback()` still has no fallback and fails `ANALYST_REPORT` outright

issues.title: Overlay cleanup still drops source website lines when TranslateGemma returns empty output
issues.summary: The overlay cleanup pipeline places `_rule_empty_output()` before `_rule_source_websites()`. When the translated overlay text is empty, cleanup returns `""` immediately instead of falling back to the source website line. The TranslateGemma compact path then returns that empty string directly, so website-only source lines can disappear from translated overlay output.
issues.impact: If the translation model returns an empty overlay string for a URL or website line, the sanitized TranslateGemma result can become blank instead of preserving the source website. That causes translated layout/overlay output to lose recoverable source text on a live translation path.
issues.notes: Overlay cleanup still drops source website lines when TranslateGemma returns empty output

issues.title: `ensure_models.py` still false-greens missing model imports and preload failures
issues.summary: The air-gap/model-readiness script logs missing imports and failed downloads, but it never converts those failures into a non-zero exit. `check_import()` returns `False` on missing modules, transformer preload failures are log-only, Surya preload failures are log-only, and the script still reaches normal completion. `manage.py check_models()` relies on the command exit status to decide whether model checks passed.
issues.impact: Operators can run the documented model-readiness check and get an apparent success even when required OCR, translation, or NER assets failed to import or preload. That makes air-gap preparation and local model validation untrustworthy at the exact point where failures should stop deployment.
issues.notes: `ensure_models.py` still false-greens missing model imports and preload failures

issues.title: `common.utils` still hard-fails imports before its MIME fallback can run
issues.summary: `common.utils` imports `magic` at module load time, but the advertised fallback to `application/octet-stream` exists only inside `detect_mime_type_from_content()`. Intake imports that helper at router import time, so a host missing `python-magic` fails while importing the module instead of degrading gracefully when MIME detection is attempted.
issues.impact: API startup or router import can fail outright on hosts where `python-magic` is absent or misinstalled, even though the helper contract says MIME detection should fall back safely. That turns an optional detection dependency into a hard import-time availability fault on a core intake surface.
issues.notes: `common.utils` still hard-fails imports before its MIME fallback can run

issues.title: Processing-handler `manifest.json` is still an unenforced false-green provenance gate
issues.summary: The repository says every new job type must declare a `manifest.json` whose schemas the dispatcher validates at startup. But the live provenance gate only checks that `worker/src/handlers/processing/manifest.json` exists, and the runtime dispatcher validates only `JOB_CONTRACTS` inputs. The manifest file’s `input_schema` and `output_schema` are never parsed or enforced.
issues.impact: The handler manifest can drift arbitrarily from real processing behavior while startup and drift checks still pass. That makes the stated provenance gate non-operative for the schemas it claims to enforce.
issues.notes: Processing-handler `manifest.json` is still an unenforced false-green provenance gate

issues.title: `model_manager` VRAM budgeting is still dead on the live model-load paths
issues.summary: `ModelManager.load_model()` only runs its VRAM-availability check and LRU eviction path when callers provide a positive `est_vram_mb`.
